result:
I find myself thinking a lot about silence lately—the varieties of it, the ways it can press itself down like warm cotton or slice clean like ice. One afternoon last summer I stepped onto a country train platform just after waking from an accidental nap; the carriage door thunked shut behind me, and an almost unnatural hush opened up, as though someone had turned the sky’s volume knob all the way to the left. A crow clacked its wings once, somewhere overhead, and the sound splintered the air like a dropped glass. I realized I had been craving that-that-breaking, the fragile hinge between stillness and motion.

Human silence is even stranger. The gaps between friends when nothing needs to be said, thick as honey but somehow weightless; the sterilizing silence that settles after an argument, sharp with all the words still hovering like fruit flies; the hush of shared awe in an empty museum gallery when your shoes squeak and you both decide to stand still and breathe like fugitives who just cracked a safe no one else will ever see.

One night, during a blackout in my city, every light vanished and the traffic signals stopped their endless mechanical blinking. People lit candles on balconies, and in that collective dim the block grew almost rural. A neighbor I’d never met played a clarinet slowly, the notes drifting across courtyards like prayer. I sat on my windowsill and felt the city speaking in its first quiet voice in years—one without sirens, engines, or neon. It sounded ancient. And I understood for a moment why monks chase silence the way astronomers chase darkness: not as an absence but as a rich, overfull presence that makes room for all the quieter truths we usually drown in noise.

I sometimes wonder what we’ve lost with our playlists always shuffling, our podcasts filling every bus seat and grocery aisle. Noise can be comfort, yes, but it can also be armor. Armor against hearing our own pulse remind us we’ve got a finite number left. Against noticing the way one person’s laugh falters a notch in the middle while they pretend they’re fine. Against the creaking universe that says, go on, pay attention.

Yet even inside anechoic chambers—those soundless rooms engineered to swallow every reflection—people report hearing the rush of blood in their ears, the click of eyelids blinking. Total silence might be a fiction, or maybe just another word for awareness sharpened to a point that pricks.

Tonight there’s wind worrying the eaves. I could drown it out with music, but instead I’m letting it have its say. Somewhere inside that wind is the breath of mountains, the hush of oceans, the sigh of continents. It’s never really quiet; we just pretend the world doesn’t speak unless it uses words like ours.
